content.problemStatement:
An N√óNN\times NN√óN matrix BBB is called **KKK-strange** if it satisfies the following conditions:-


Each integer from 111 to N2N^2N2 appears exactly once in BBB; and
If every element of BBB is reduced modulo KKK, the resulting matrix is symmetric.

Formally, that means Bi,j‚Äämod‚ÄäK=Bj,i‚Äämod‚ÄäKB_{i, j} \bmod K = B_{j, i} \bmod KBi,j‚ÄãmodK=Bj,i‚ÄãmodK for every 1‚â§i,j‚â§N1 \leq i, j \leq N1‚â§i,j‚â§N; where x‚Äämod‚Ääyx\bmod yxmody denotes the remainder obtained upon dividing xxx by yyy.
For example, [4132]\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 3 & 2\end{bmatrix}[43‚Äã12‚Äã] is both a 111-strange and a 222-strange matrix, but is not a 333-strange matrix.

Meanwhile, [1523]\begin{bmatrix}1 & 5 \\ 2 & 3\end{bmatrix}[12‚Äã53‚Äã] is not KKK-strange for any KKK, since it contains an entry larger than N2=4N^2 = 4N2=4; and [1112]\begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2\end{bmatrix}[11‚Äã12‚Äã] is also not KKK-strange for any KKK since it contains repeated elements.

Let MNM_NMN‚Äã be the **largest integer** for which there exists an MNM_NMN‚Äã-strange N√óNN\times NN√óN matrix.

Sahil has with him an N√óNN\times NN√óN matrix AAA, but some of its elements are missing.

Each element of AAA is either an integer between 111 and N2N^2N2, or 000 (denoting a missing element).

Find the number of ways to fill in the zeros with integers, so that the resulting matrix is MNM_NMN‚Äã-strange.

This number can be large, so print it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the dimension of the matrix.
The next NNN lines describe the matrix. The iii-th of them contains NNN space-separated integers Ai,1,Ai,2,‚Ä¶,Ai,NA_{i, 1}, A_{i, 2}, \ldots, A_{i, N}Ai,1‚Äã,Ai,2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,Ai,N‚Äã, denoting the elements in the iii-th row of the matrix.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of ways to fill in the zeros of AAA such that the resulting matrix is MNM_NMN‚Äã-strange, modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: All the non-zero Ai,jA_{i, j}Ai,j‚Äã values are distinct.
The sum of N2N^2N2 over all test cases won't exceed 5‚ãÖ1055\cdot 10^55‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: For N=1N = 1N=1, we have M1=1M_1 = 1M1‚Äã=1, since the only strange matrix is [1][1][1] itself.
There's only one way to fill in AAA to get a strange matrix.
Test case 222: For N=2N = 2N=2, it can be shown that M2=3M_2 = 3M2‚Äã=3 (for example, with the matrix [3412]\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}[31‚Äã42‚Äã]).
There's no way to fill in the given AAA to make it 333-strange though, so the answer is 000.
